title: Caribbean countries are feeling the squeeze from this energy crisis—and not just from gas prices
gist: At the Atlantic Council Global Energy Forum, Barbados energy minister Kerrie Symmonds and Suriname oil/gas minister Patrick Brunings describe a Caribbean 'tightening trilemma': a Strait of Hormuz-driven oil spike (with Barbados subsidizing gasoline) on top of shrinking climate-adaptation finance as donor nations turn inward to secure their own energy. Both pitch hydrocarbons as a bridge to fund renewables—Suriname ramping offshore oil from 16kbd to 400-500kbd by 2031 and gas by 2030 as a regional supplier, Barbados pushing the Bridgetown Initiative to rewrite the global financial architecture with climate-resilient debt clauses.

themes.theme: Hormuz oil shock hitting import-dependent SIDS

insights.claim: Barbados is subsidizing gasoline to hold pump prices at $4.01/gallon (down from ~$4.70) to absorb the oil-price spike from the ongoing Strait of Hormuz holdup, but says the subsidy 'cannot continue indefinitely.'
insights.speaker: Katherine Walla
insights.so_what: A Hormuz-driven oil spike is already forcing emerging-market fiscal subsidies that have a near-term expiry—watch for subsidy rollbacks feeding CPI and sovereign stress in small importers.
insights.quote: the government of Barbados has essentially been applying a subsidy to gasoline, bringing it from around $4.70 a gallon to $4.01. But “that cannot continue indefinitely.”
insights.locator: At the Global Energy Forum on Tuesday

insights.claim: Barbados imports ~90% of its electricity generation from fossil fuels, and the 2022 Ukraine supply disruption tripled its annual petroleum import bill from ~$400 million to $1.3 billion in a single year.
insights.speaker: Kerrie Symmonds
insights.so_what: Quantifies how directly global oil volatility hits an import-dependent grid—a 3x import-bill swing is the kind of shock that drives the fuel-adjustment clause and cost-of-living crises in net energy importers.
insights.quote: On average, we would import petroleum products into Barbados at a cost of $400 million per year, and when the Ukraine war started and there was the disruption of supply, we were at a stage where for a year or two we were spending $1.3 billion in United States currency.
insights.locator: For an island like Barbados, we are effectively a net energy importer

insights.claim: Caribbean electricity costs run 29-40 cents/kWh versus a ~15 cents/kWh US average, which Symmonds frames not as a charity case but as a high-return market for renewable investors given aggressive decarbonization targets.
insights.speaker: Kerrie Symmonds
insights.so_what: A 2-3x power-price premium over the US is the explicit return hook for utility-scale solar and storage developers willing to underwrite Caribbean grid projects.
insights.quote: it is characterized by extremely high energy costs, on average from twenty-nine cents to forty cents per kilowatt hour, as compared to an average of fifteen cents per kilowatt hour here in the United States of America.
insights.locator: Let me pause there to make another point abundantly clear

themes.theme: The tightening trilemma: shrinking adaptation finance

insights.claim: As major emitters turn inward to secure their own energy and modernize domestic grids, the climate-adaptation finance the Caribbean has relied on from those donors is being cut off, creating a 'critical time window' before investment returns.
insights.speaker: Patrick Brunings
insights.so_what: Energy-security nationalism in rich countries directly drains climate-adaptation flows to vulnerable states—a downstream geopolitical consequence to watch as defense/energy budgets crowd out aid.
insights.quote: Donor countries are turning inwards, prioritizing their own energy security, grid modernization, and domestic investment in renewables. This can lead to a critical time window before investments will return to the Caribbean region again.
insights.locator: The low-lying coastal islands in the Caribbean have counted on financial support

insights.claim: Brunings frames the Caribbean as caught in a 'tightening trilemma' of rising climate impacts, shrinking adaptation finance, and a stressed global energy system converging exactly when resilience is most needed.
insights.speaker: Patrick Brunings
insights.so_what: A clean three-vector frame (climate cost up, finance down, energy stressed) for assessing tail risk and reconstruction-funding gaps across small island sovereigns.
insights.quote: the Caribbean region faces a tightening trilemma: rising climate impacts, shrinking adaptation finance, and a global energy system under pressure, all converging at the moment when resilience is most urgently needed.
insights.locator: In effect, the Caribbean region faces a tightening trilemma

insights.claim: Hurricane Melissa, which struck Jamaica in October, caused $12.2 billion in total damage and losses—instantly wiping out over 56% of Jamaica's annual economic output from a single event—and even fully-deployed cat bonds, parametric insurance, and contingent credit left an unmitigated multibillion-dollar reconstruction gap.
insights.speaker: Kerrie Symmonds
insights.so_what: Concrete proof that even best-in-class disaster-risk-financing instruments are structurally undersized for a single mega-storm—relevant for cat-bond / parametric insurance pricing and sovereign credit on climate-exposed islands.
insights.quote: total damage and losses from Melissa reached a staggering $12.2 billion (with a B) USD, but in Jamaica’s case, it instantaneously wiped out over 56 percent of Jamaica’s annual economic output from that single climatic event.
insights.locator: as recently as last year when Hurricane Melissa passed through

themes.theme: Hydrocarbons as the bridge to finance renewables

insights.claim: Symmonds argues SIDS should exploit domestic hydrocarbon prospectivity through 2050—anchored on the IEA roadmap's projected 20% residual fossil-fuel use by 2050—to self-finance their renewable-energy commitments, with the US (under renewed oil-and-gas focus) as a potential off-taker.
insights.speaker: Kerrie Symmonds
insights.so_what: 'Transition has become addition'—even decarbonization-committed island states are now planning new oil/gas to fund the transition, an underappreciated source of incremental hydrocarbon supply and a market for US exports.
insights.quote: the opportunity exists between now and 2050 to exploit and explore those hydrocarbons in the context of being able to finance our way to the renewable energy commitments which we have made.
insights.locator: But there is a solution which we are trying to evolve

insights.claim: Brunings declares 'transition has become addition,' arguing AI's exponential power growth cannot be met by renewables alone and that Ukraine and Middle East wars have pushed countries back toward securing their own energy mixes.
insights.speaker: Patrick Brunings
insights.so_what: A frontline policymaker confirming the consensus shift: AI load growth plus war-driven security anxiety mean fossil demand is additive, not substitutive—supportive of sustained gas demand.
insights.quote: The world is realizing that additional energy for the exponential growth of AI cannot be supplied by renewable energy alone. This has been said throughout this summit. Transition has become addition.
insights.locator: We are again at another crossroad due to the recent global developments

insights.claim: Brunings positions gas as both a transition and a 'destination' fuel that will be 'the backbone of the energy mixes for many countries,' buying Suriname and the region time to build clean energy.
insights.speaker: Patrick Brunings
insights.so_what: Gas-as-destination-fuel framing from a soon-to-be producer signals long-dated regional gas demand rather than a near-term peak—relevant for LNG/gas infrastructure theses.
insights.quote: gas can be regarded as a transition fuel and even as a destination fuel, and will be the backbone of the energy mixes for many countries around the world.
insights.locator: In the transition towards the Suriname 3.0

insights.claim: Suriname's oil output, based on offshore discoveries since 2019, is set to soar from 16,000 bpd to 220,000 bpd by 2028 and 400,000-500,000 bpd by 2031, with gas reaching 150 million cubic feet/day by 2030—for a population of just 600,000.
insights.speaker: Patrick Brunings
insights.so_what: A 25-30x oil-production ramp in a 600k-person country signals a major new non-OPEC supply source and an extreme per-capita petro-windfall—watch execution timelines (first oil/gas) and partner operators.
insights.quote: Onshore production will soar from 16,000 barrels of oil per day to 220,000 barrels oil per day by 2028, up to 400,000 or even a half million barrels oil per day in 2031. Gas will reach a production of 150 million cubic feet per day by 2030.
insights.locator: In two years’ time, Suriname will be also one of the three countries

insights.claim: Suriname abandoned traditional bid rounds in November and launched an 'open-door' offering across roughly 50% of its still-open offshore acreage, where any company can bid on a self-defined area with the original submitter holding right of first refusal.
insights.speaker: Patrick Brunings
insights.so_what: A novel continuous open-acreage licensing model lowers the entry barrier for E&P explorers—an actionable opening for upstream players seeking Guyana-Suriname basin exposure.
insights.quote: Suriname stepped away from the traditional bid rounds and launched an open-door offering in the open acreage where any company can submit a bid over an area with associated work program.
insights.locator: Almost 50 percent of the offshore acres of Suriname is still available

insights.claim: Suriname intends to remain one of only three carbon-negative countries (via ~93% rainforest and mangrove cover) even as it becomes a major oil producer, monetizing the carbon sink through high-value carbon credits (ITMOs) funded by oil-and-gas revenues.
insights.speaker: Patrick Brunings
insights.so_what: A producer explicitly pairing oil revenue with tradable ITMO carbon credits is a model for 'carbon-negative petrostate' branding and a real demand source for high-integrity Article 6 credits.
insights.quote: Maintaining this negative status would require investments, and we will do so by making use of trading our high value carbon credits, our ITMOs, and using the oil and gas revenues.
insights.locator: Suriname has taken the position that although we will continue to develop

insights.claim: Suriname is also evaluating frontier supply options including solar, wind, hydro (off a constant east-west Gulf Stream), geothermal, small modular reactors, and 'out of the box' plays like white/geologic hydrogen and helium-3 occurrences in its basin.
insights.speaker: Patrick Brunings
insights.so_what: Early-stage interest in SMRs, geologic hydrogen and helium-3 from a cash-rich emerging producer flags speculative optionality—worth tracking for frontier-tech partnership announcements.
insights.quote: Suriname will also look into more out of the box technologies like white hydrogen, sort of so-called geologic hydrogen, and even helium-3 occurrences in our basin
insights.locator: Looking forward and inwards, the decision will be made this summer

themes.theme: Bridgetown Initiative: rewiring global financial architecture

insights.claim: Symmonds argues the real bottleneck for Caribbean energy resilience is not sunshine, wind, or fiscal discipline but the design of the global financial architecture, which lacks the systemic liquidity needed to fund grid modernization and utility-scale solar.
insights.speaker: Kerrie Symmonds
insights.so_what: Reframes the climate-finance gap as an allocation/architecture problem ('the capital exists')—the political case for MDB reform and a signal of where reform pressure (and dealflow) will concentrate.
insights.quote: the problem at this stage is not the fiscal discipline of individual nations in and of themselves, but rather that the problem is an inadequacy of design of the global financial architecture.
insights.locator: It is conclusive proof that individual state readiness is no longer enough

insights.claim: The Bridgetown Initiative proposes embedding climate-resilient debt clauses into all sovereign loan agreements that automatically pause debt servicing for two years after a verified disaster, preventing an environmental crisis from becoming an insolvency crisis.
insights.speaker: Kerrie Symmonds
insights.so_what: Automatic disaster-triggered debt pauses would reprice climate-exposed sovereign debt—watch adoption in new EM sovereign issuance and its effect on creditor recovery assumptions.
insights.quote: In the event of a catastrophe or a verifiable natural disaster, debt servicing is automatically paused for two years, and this immediately frees up international liquidity to focus on recovery and on reconstruction, preventing an environmental crisis from becoming an insolvency crisis.
insights.locator: First, we have to require the systemic integration of climate resilient debt clauses

insights.claim: The initiative calls on multilateral development banks to scale up third-party risk guarantees, blended finance, and FX hedging to absorb macro-level risk and lower entry barriers so institutional investors can fund bankable Caribbean grid infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Kerrie Symmonds
insights.so_what: Demand for MDB risk-guarantees and FX hedging defines the de-risking structures private capital will need before committing to EM grid assets—a roadmap for blended-finance deal structuring.
insights.quote: we need multilateral development banks to scale up third-party risk guarantees and also to scale up blended finance structures and foreign exchange hedging mechanisms.
insights.locator: Secondly, we are aiming to bridge the cost-of-capital gap

insights.claim: Symmonds cites the COP28 global declaration on debt-for-nature swaps as formal acknowledgment that high sovereign debt and climate vulnerability must be tackled together, with refinancing savings legally ring-fenced into dedicated resilience funds rather than general expenditure.
insights.speaker: Kerrie Symmonds
insights.so_what: Legally earmarking debt-swap savings into resilience funds is the structural detail that makes these instruments investable and auditable—a template for future debt-for-nature deal terms.
insights.quote: The critical policy distinction here and the blueprint we are advancing is that the resulting fiscal savings do not disappear into general expenditure. They are rather legally refinanced and channeled directly into dedicated resilience funds.
insights.locator: At COP28, the global community formally acknowledged this reality

insights.claim: Brunings argues oil-producing Caribbean states (Trinidad, Guyana, Suriname) should deliver cheap oil and gas to non-producing SIDS during the critical finance-gap window and pre-finance their energy self-sufficiency—on partnership terms, not charity.
insights.speaker: Patrick Brunings
insights.so_what: Intra-regional energy supply pacts could redirect some Suriname/Guyana output to neighbors—worth watching as a captive regional off-take channel separate from export markets.
insights.quote: Collaboration should result in delivering cheap oil and gas to the non-oil producing SIDS countries during this critical time window and pre-financing in technologies for these countries to become energy sufficient.
insights.locator: Suriname, Guyana, and Trinidad can and should work out

insights.claim: Symmonds calls for a region-wide regulatory system and pooled procurement of renewable-energy technologies so Caribbean states can use scale to bring costs down.
insights.speaker: Kerrie Symmonds
insights.so_what: Harmonized regional regulation plus aggregated procurement would create larger, more bankable tenders—a potential efficiency unlock for renewable-tech suppliers serving the region.
insights.quote: he suggested that Caribbean countries share the effort to procure renewable energy technologies, since by increasing the scale of the efforts, the countries can “bring costs down.”
insights.locator: Symmonds called for a “region-wide” regulatory system
